evidence: The generation-task seam uses mixed failure contracts: `resolveGenerationProjectScope` returns status objects (`query_failed`, `missing_generation`), while `getPrimaryTaskMappingsForGenerations` throws on query failure, forcing callers to branch by API shape instead of domain state (`src/shared/lib/generationTaskRepository.ts`).

The same `tasks` database field is translated differently across boundaries: repository parser marks non-array as `invalid_tasks_shape`, but generation transformers accept `string | string[]` and coerce first item (`src/shared/lib/generationTaskRepository.ts`, `src/shared/lib/generationTransformers.ts`).

Invalidation orchestration keeps global module state (`scheduledInvalidations`, `invalidationTrace`) and dedupes only by `shot:${id}` / `generation:${id}`, creating cross-scope coupling risk when multiple projects/query clients are active (`src/shared/hooks/invalidation/useGenerationInvalidation.ts`).

Several boundary modules in this batch do not have direct companion tests discoverable by filename/pattern search (notably `generationTaskRepository`, `operationResult`, `taskViewContract`, `generationMetadata`), while adjacent integrators are tested, leaving seam behavior under-validated.

findings.summary: Generation-task repository APIs expose inconsistent error handoff styles (status-return vs throw), making integration seams unpredictable.
evidence: `resolveGenerationProjectScope` encodes failures in return status (`query_failed`, `missing_generation`) while `getPrimaryTaskMappingsForGenerations` throws `Error` on query failure.
`preloadGenerationTaskMappings` must wrap repository calls in `try/catch` and continue after runtime reporting, while other consumers rely on thrown errors propagating through React Query hooks.
findings.suggestion: Unify repository boundary semantics to one contract style (prefer `OperationResult`/typed status for recoverable DB issues), then adapt cache/hook callers to consume a single predictable failure channel.

findings.summary: Different translators interpret the same `tasks` field shape differently, causing cross-module data contract drift.
evidence: `extractTaskId` in transformers accepts both `string` and `string[]` and silently normalizes to first ID.
This means the same row can be considered valid in UI transform flow but invalid in task-mapping flow.
findings.suggestion: Define one canonical parser for `tasks` shape in a shared boundary utility and reuse it in repository, cache preload, and transformers so status and displayed task linkage stay aligned.

findings.summary: Invalidation scheduling uses process-global mutable maps keyed only by entity ID, coupling unrelated lifecycles across contexts.
evidence: Dedupe keys omit query-client/project scope (`shot:${shotId}`, `generation:${generationId}`), so identical IDs from different contexts can overwrite timers/traces.
Timeline write queue similarly centralizes per-shot serialization, reinforcing hidden global coordination expectations.
findings.suggestion: Scope invalidation scheduler state by `QueryClient` + project (or inject a scheduler instance) and include scope identifiers in dedupe keys to prevent accidental cross-context interference.

findings.summary: Several boundary-contract utilities in this batch lack direct tests, leaving API consistency regressions likely.
evidence: Test discovery in `src` found direct tests for some neighboring seams (`adjacentPagePreloadCoordinator`, `timelineWriteQueue`, `useLineageChain`, `cacheUtils`) but no direct test files for these listed contract modules by filename/pattern.
These modules define normalization and boundary contracts consumed across features, so lack of direct seam tests increases integration fragility.
findings.suggestion: Add focused unit tests for contract behavior (shape coercion, invalid input handling, versioned contract fields, error metadata conversion) and cross-module compatibility tests for repository-to-transformer expectations.
